Summary: This study explores the use of LI-RADS and multiparametric MRI radiomics in predicting microvascular invasion (MVI) in patients with solitary hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) < 5 cm. The study found that incomplete tumor capsule and corona enhancement were significantly related to MVI, and a combination model incorporating these features with radiomic features from multiple MRI sequences improved MVI prediction compared to clinical or single-sequence radiomic models alone. The combination model achieved an AUC of 0.858 in an independent test cohort.

Condition1.reason: The study includes tumor segmentation for radiomic feature extraction, as stated in the 'Tumor Segmentation' subsection of the Methods.

Condition2.reason: The tumor segmentation was performed manually by two radiologists, so it is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features using PyRadiomics, which involves hand-crafted feature extraction.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data, as the extracted radiomic features are numerical and organized for feature selection and model building.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted radiomic features and traditional machine learning algorithms like AdaBoost and logistic regression.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study defines clear inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria are detailed in the 'Patients' section of the Methods.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathological confirmation of HCC and MVI status, which is a high-quality reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study was conducted at a single institution.

Item5.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced MRI, which is a clinically translatable imaging technique for HCC diagnosis and staging. LI-RADS is also used.

Item6.reason: The study reports the imaging protocol with acquisition parameters in the 'MRI Technology' section, including TR, TE, flip angle, FOV, matrix, section thickness, and contrast agent details.

Item7.reason: The study specifies that CE-MRI examinations were performed within 30 days preoperatively, which is appropriate for validating the presence or absence of MVI at the time of imaging.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of 3D-ROI drawn along the tumor margin layer by layer by two radiologists. Preprocessing steps like voxel resampling and gray-level discretization are also mentioned.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by two radiologists, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The tumor segmentation was performed by two radiologists, and in cases of disagreement, a senior radiologist was consulted to make a final decision. This reflects clinical practice.

Item11.reason: The study describes the image preprocessing techniques used, including voxel resampling to 1x1x1 mm3 using B-spline interpolation and gray-level intensity discretization with a fixed bin width of 25. This is appropriate for the imaging modality and feature extraction techniques used.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics v.2.1.2 for feature extraction, which is a standardized software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomic features) and categories (first-order statistics, GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, NGTDM, GLDM). It also mentions that the same series of radiomic features were extracted from T2WI/FS, AP, PVP, and DP images.

Item14.reason: The study performs ICC analysis to assess the reliability and stability of each feature, retaining only those with ICC values greater than 0.75. This addresses the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study uses Pearson's correlation to remove redundant features with mutual correlation coefficients > 0.9, univariate statistical difference analysis to identify discriminative features, and LASSO for further feature selection. This addresses the removal of redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical justification for the number of instances and features used in the final training dataset.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that patients were stratified into training, internal validation, and independent test cohorts. However, it does not explicitly state whether the data split was done at the very beginning of the analysis pipeline or whether a fixed random seed was used. It also does not mention whether the data split was on the patient level, not the scan level.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the analysis and handling of potential confounding factors like different distributions of patient characteristics across sites or scanners.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. ROC curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis (DCA) are also used to assess model performance.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values, which provides a measure of uncertainty.

Item22.reason: The study assesses the calibration of the final models using calibration curves.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform uni-parametric evaluations to justify the need for a multi-parametric approach. It uses multiple MRI sequences without comparing their individual performance to the combined performance.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based models with a clinical model based on LI-RADS features and other imaging features. It also uses the Delong test to compare the AUC values of different models.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the performance of the models with a simple baseline reference model like a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an internal validation cohort.

Item27.reason: The model is tested on an independent test cohort.

Item28.reason: The study does not mention any data being shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention any code being shared with the public.
